one_line_takeaway: MMCoA extends CLIP adversarial training to jointly harden the text encoder via dual cross-modal contrastive losses, winning under multimodal/text attacks on class-rich data -- but the paper honestly shows text-attack robustness under large distribution shift is still an open problem.
key_finding: MMCoA delivers the strongest robustness under multimodal and text attacks, especially on class-rich datasets, while nearly matching the image-only baseline TeCoA under pure image attack. In-distribution under multimodal attack it reaches 84.52% (CIFAR10) and 30.02% (ImageNet) vs TeCoA's 79.53% / 11.96% and CLIP's 11.65% / 1.80%. A central diagnostic insight: image attacks are generally more potent than text attacks, but text attacks strengthen as the number of classes grows (CIFAR10->CIFAR100->TinyImageNet->ImageNet), and TeCoA (image-only defense) NEVER beats zero-shot CLIP's average robust accuracy under text attack across settings. The paper also reports that under minimal distribution shift robust and clean accuracy are positively correlated (no classic trade-off), whereas under large distribution shift the trade-off returns.
core_contribution: The paper proposes MMCoA (Multimodal Contrastive Adversarial training), an adversarial-training framework that hardens BOTH the image and text encoders of CLIP-style VLMs against image, text, and combined multimodal attacks. Its own novelty over prior work (which the paper explicitly contrasts with the cited AdvPT and TeCoA baselines that defend only the image encoder) is a dual cross-modal contrastive objective: a text-supervised image adversarial loss (L_{I'-T}) that aligns adversarial IMAGE embeddings with clean TEXT embeddings, plus an image-supervised text adversarial loss (L_{I-T'}) that aligns adversarial TEXT embeddings with clean IMAGE embeddings, combined as L = gamma1*L_{I'-T} + gamma2*L_{I-T'} (both gamma set to 0.5). The paper frames itself as the 'first comprehensive study' of VLM robustness spanning all three attack modalities and delivers an empirical characterization across 15 datasets and two tasks (in-distribution and out-of-distribution/zero-shot).
threat_model: White-box adversarial evasion against CLIP-based VLMs used for zero-shot / fine-tuned classification and image-text retrieval. The attacker can perturb (a) the image via PGD (L_infinity or L_2 ball; the paper uses L_infinity epsilon=1/255, 100 PGD steps at test time), (b) the text via BERT-Attack token substitution/insertion/deletion (max 1-token perturbation, candidate word-list length 10, targeting class tokens), or (c) both simultaneously ('multimodal attack' = PGD image + BERT-Attack text, and the stronger interacting Co-Attack). The attacker's goal is to break cross-modal alignment so the model misclassifies or retrieves the wrong item. The defender is assumed to control training and can adversarially fine-tune CLIP (full fine-tuning of both encoders) on a labeled dataset before deployment. Attacks are white-box by default.
limitations: By the paper's OWN statements: (1) defending against text attacks under large text-distribution shifts remains unsolved -- none of the methods (including MMCoA) reliably beat CLIP under text attack in the OOD few-shot regime, explicitly left as future work in the Limitations section. (2) Combining both losses actually WEAKENS text-attack defense relative to using the image-supervised text loss alone (ablation Table IV), i.e. aligning adversarial images with clean text hurts text robustness. (3) Under image attack with abundant (full-shot) OOD data, image-only TeCoA can slightly outperform MMCoA. (4) MMCoA is 'more challenging to fit' and needs more epochs (28 reported vs TeCoA's 10) to converge. (5) Only the CLIP ViT-B/32 backbone is evaluated; no larger CLIP or non-CLIP VLM. (6) Threat evaluation is primarily white-box; transfer/black-box robustness is not the focus.

quantitative_results: In-distribution multimodal attack, CIFAR10 robust acc: MMCoA 84.52 vs TeCoA 79.53 vs FT-standard 64.39 vs CLIP 11.65
In-distribution multimodal attack, ImageNet robust acc: MMCoA 30.02 vs TeCoA 11.96 vs FT-standard 3.47 vs CLIP 1.80
In-distribution text attack, ImageNet robust acc: MMCoA 41.34 vs TeCoA 15.84 vs CLIP 13.65
In-distribution image attack, TinyImageNet robust acc: MMCoA 53.97 vs TeCoA 49.01 vs CLIP 1.93
MMCoA vs CLIP in-distribution gains: ~45-75% (image), 16-38% (text), 28-72% (multimodal) across the 4 datasets
OOD full-shot multimodal attack, 15-dataset average robust acc: MMCoA 22.00 vs TeCoA 19.96 vs FT-standard 7.23 vs CLIP 4.64
OOD 50-shot multimodal attack average: MMCoA 18.46 vs TeCoA 15.00 vs FT-standard 6.89 vs CLIP 4.64
Co-Attack in-distribution CIFAR100 robust acc: MMCoA 60.40 vs TeCoA 48.13 vs CLIP 9.17
Co-Attack OOD 50-shot average robust acc: MMCoA 22.66 vs TeCoA 15.42 vs FT-standard 17.70 vs CLIP 13.47
MSCOCO retrieval under Co-Attack, image-to-text R@1: MMCoA 13.22 vs TeCoA 10.94 vs CLIP 3.94; text-to-image R@1: MMCoA 7.39 vs TeCoA 7.40 vs CLIP 1.73

Ablation (Table IV): image-supervised text loss ALONE gives best text-attack robustness (e.g. CIFAR10 96.74) exceeding the full dual-loss MMCoA (96.16), showing the two losses trade off on text robustness
Attack settings: train 10-step PGD epsilon=1/255 L_infinity; test 100-step PGD epsilon=1/255; BERT-Attack 1-token, word-list length 10
t-SNE clean-vs-adversarial feature cosine similarity on CIFAR10: MMCoA 0.907 > TeCoA 0.856 > CLIP 0.795
baselines_compared: Zero-shot CLIP (ViT-B/32, prompt 'This is a photo of a [CLS]', no fine-tuning)
FT-standard (both encoders fine-tuned on clean data)
TeCoA (text-guided contrastive adversarial training, image-encoder defense; the primary SOTA baseline)
VP (visual prompting for adversarial robustness, appendix)
